---
title: "gov51-ps2"
author: "Xihe"
date: "2026-03-06"
output: html_document
---

```{r}
## git repository URL: https://github.com/Xihe-Helena-Tang/gov51-ps2.git


```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:
```{r}
##1.1 Exploring the Experiment
##1.1.1 Load packages and data
# Your code here
library(tidyverse)
gay <- read_csv("data/raw/gay.csv")


##Q1.1 (3 points): How many total observations are in gay.csv? How many unique values does study take? List all the treatment conditions in the treatment variable.
#answers: There are total 69592 observations in gay.csv. the study takes two unique values. Treatment variable list: 
"No Contact"                                    
"Recycling Script by Gay Canvasser"             
"Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser"     
"Recycling Script by Straight Canvasser"        
"Same-Sex Marriage Script by Straight Canvasser"

# Your code here
nrow(gay)

unique(gay$study)

unique(gay$treatment)




```


```{r}


##Q1.2 (3 points): Filter to Study 1, Wave 1 only. Calculate the mean of ssm (same-sex marriage attitude, scored 1–5) for each treatment group using group_by() and summarize(). Which treatment group shows the highest average support?
##Answer: the "Recycling Script by Gay Canvasser" treatment group shows the highest average support, which is 3.13.
  
# Your code here
  
  gay_study1_wv1 <- gay|>
  filter(study == 1, wave == 1)|>
  group_by(treatment)|>
  summarise(mean_ssm = mean(ssm, na.rm = TRUE))

  print(gay_study1_wv1)


##Q1.3 (3 points): Still using Study 1, Wave 1, calculate the standard deviation of ssm within each treatment group. Are the standard deviations roughly similar across groups? Why is this important in a randomized experiment? (2–3 sentences)
##Answer: the standerd diviation is roughly similar across groups, which is around 1.67-1.70. This is important in a randomized experiment, because the variance of standard deviations across treatment groups indicates that there are other confounders disturbing the outcomes. Similar SD suggests that the groups are balanced before treatment and that randomization worked properly.
  gay|>
  filter(study == 1, wave == 1)|>
  group_by(treatment)|>
  summarise(sd_ssm = sd(ssm, na.rm = TRUE))

# Your code here
#Your interpretation:
##Q1.4 (3 points): Create a bar plot of the mean ssm by treatment group (Study 1, Wave 1) using ggplot() with geom_col(). Hint: First create a summary data frame with the means, then plot it.
# Your code here
  
  ggplot(gay_study1_wv1, aes(x = treatment, y = mean_ssm)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Support for Same-Sex Marriage",
    x = "Treatment Group",
    y = "Mean SSM"
  )

  theme(
  plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
  axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10)
)


##Q1.5 (4 points): A well-run randomized experiment should produce groups that look similar at baseline. Filter gay.csv to Study 1, Wave 1. Compare the “Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser” group and the “No Contact” group: • Calculate the mean and SD of ssm for each group • Compute the standardized difference in means:̄𝑥gay canvasser− ̄𝑥no contact√(𝑠2gay canvasser+𝑠2 no contact)/2. A  standardized difference below 0.25 in absolute value suggests good balance. Do these groups look similar at baseline? (2–3 sentences)
##interpretation: These groups look similar at baseline. The standardized difference is -0.01, which is way below 0.25 in absolute value, suggesting that the treatment and control groups are in good balance.The randomization seems to be very successful.
# Your code here
  balance <- gay |>
  filter(
    study == 1,
    wave == 1,
    treatment %in% c(
      "Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser",
      "No Contact"
    )
  ) |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(
    mean_ssm = mean(ssm, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_ssm = sd(ssm, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

print(balance)

mean_gay <- balance$mean_ssm[
  balance$treatment == "Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser"
]

mean_no <- balance$mean_ssm[
  balance$treatment == "No Contact"
]

sd_gay <- balance$sd_ssm[
  balance$treatment == "Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser"
]

sd_no <- balance$sd_ssm[
  balance$treatment == "No Contact"
]

sd_diff <- (mean_gay - mean_no) / sqrt((sd_gay^2 + sd_no^2) / 2)

print(sd_diff)

  
##Q1.6 (4 points) 

#Your interpretation: The difference is 0.10. As the treatment groups are balanced at the baseline, the difference suggests that the treatment had some effects on the treatment group. So the conversation with gay canvasser increases support for same-sex marriage.

 # Your code here
gay_study1_wv2 <- gay |>
  filter(
    study == 1,
    wave == 2,
    treatment %in% c(
      "Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser",
      "No Contact"
    )
  ) |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(mean_ssm = mean(ssm, na.rm = TRUE))

print(gay_study1_wv2)

mean_gay_w2 <- gay_study1_wv2$mean_ssm[
  gay_study1_wv2$treatment == "Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser"
]

mean_no_w2 <- gay_study1_wv2$mean_ssm[
  gay_study1_wv2$treatment == "No Contact"
]

sd_w2 <- mean_gay_w2 - mean_no_w2

print(sd_w2)




```



```{r}


##2.1 The Baseline Distribution Q2.1 (3 points): Load gayreshaped.csv and ccap2012.csv. Report the number of observations and column names for each dataset. In gayreshaped.csv, the variables therm1 through therm4 represent feeling thermometer scores (0–100) toward gay men and lesbians, measured at each survey wave.
#Answer: the number of observations is 11948 in gay_r, and 43998 in ccap. The column names for gay_r are "study"     "treatment" "therm1"    "therm2"    "therm3"    "therm4" , and the column names for ccap are "caseid"   "gaytherm". 
# Your code here
gay_r <- read_csv("data/raw/gayreshaped.csv")
ccap <- read_csv("data/raw/ccap2012.csv")

nrow(gay_r)
names(gay_r)

nrow(ccap)
names(ccap)

##Q2.2 (5 points): Extract the baseline feeling thermometer (therm1) for Study 1 from gayreshaped.csv. Calculate its mean, median, and standard deviation. Then do the same for gaytherm from ccap2012.csv (remove NAs). Report both sets of statistics side by side. What do you notice? (1–2 sentences)
# Your code here


study1_therm <- gay_r |>
  filter(study == 1)|>
  summarise(
    mean = mean(therm1, na.rm = TRUE), 
    median = median(therm1, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = sd(therm1, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

study1_therm

ccap_therm <- ccap |>
  summarise(
    mean = mean(gaytherm, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(gaytherm, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = sd(gaytherm, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

ccap_therm
#What do you notice? The mean, median, and SD between study1 and ccap data is extremely similar, although these are two completely separate independent dataset. This suggests that study1 is suspicious of copying the data from CCAP.

  
##Q2.3 (5 points): Create side-by-side histograms comparing the LaCour Study 1 baseline (therm1) and the CCAP data (gaytherm). Use the same bin breaks for both: breaks = seq(0, 100, by = 5). Hint: Combine the two datasets into one data frame with a column indicating the source, then use ggplot() with facet_wrap(). Do these look like they came from different populations? (2–3 sentences)
# Your code here 

lacour <- gay_r |>
  filter(study == 1) |>
  select(value = therm1) |>
  mutate(source = "LaCour Study")

ccap_data <- ccap |>
  select(value = gaytherm) |>
  mutate(source = "CCAP")

combined <- bind_rows(lacour, ccap_data)

ggplot(combined, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(breaks = seq(0, 100, by = 5)) +
  facet_wrap(~source) +
  labs(
    x = "Feeling Thermometer",
    y = "Count",
    title = "Comparison of LaCour Study and CCAP Thermometer Distributions"
  )
  
#Your interpretation: The shape of LaCour Study and CCAP data is exactly the same in human eyes. This is the visualization of fraud evidence.They do not look like coming from different population at all. 

##Q2.4 (6 points): For both the Study 1 and Study 2 control groups (“No Contact”), calculate: (a) The correlation between therm1 and therm2 (use cor() with use = "complete.obs") (b) The standard deviation of the within-person change (therm2 - therm1) Present your results side by side. A re-test correlation of 0.95–0.97 is typical for real feeling thermometer data. Which study shows a more plausible pattern, and why? (3–4 sentences)
# Your code here

study1_control <- gay_r |>
  filter(study == 1, treatment == "No Contact")

cor_study1 <- cor(
  study1_control$therm1,
  study1_control$therm2,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

sd_change_study1 <- sd(
  study1_control$therm2 - study1_control$therm1,
  na.rm = TRUE
)

cor_study1
sd_change_study1

study2_control <- gay_r |>
  filter(study == 2, treatment == "No Contact")

cor_study2 <- cor(
  study2_control$therm1,
  study2_control$therm2,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

sd_change_study2 <- sd(
  study2_control$therm2 - study2_control$therm1,
  na.rm = TRUE
)

cor_study2
sd_change_study2
#Your interpretation: the retest correlation in LaCour's study1 is almost 1.00, but the correlation in study2 is 0.97. Plus, the SD in individual change is greater in study2 than that in study1, while that in study1 is extremely small. In other words, the variations in retest and individual changes in study1 are too tiny. The data in study1 seems too perfect to be true, while data from study2 looks more natural. 


##Q2.5 (5 points): Create two scatter plots side by side: (a) Study 1 control group: therm1 (x-axis) vs therm2 (y-axis), with a 45-degree line (b) Study 2 control group: therm1 (x-axis) vs therm2 (y-axis), with a 45-degree line Describe the differences. Which looks more like real longitudinal data, and why? (3–4 sentences)
# Your code here: 

scatter_data <- gay_r |>
  filter(treatment == "No Contact", study %in% c(1,2))

ggplot(scatter_data,
       aes(x = therm1, y = therm2)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1,
              color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~study) +
  labs(
    x = "Thermometer Wave 1",
    y = "Thermometer Wave 2",
    title = "Retest Thermometer Responses"
  )
#Your interpretation:Study2 looks more like real longitudinal data. There is almost no deviation of points in study1, and most points stack at the same typical numbers, such as 0, 50, 100. This is unrealistically clean. In study2, the points are scattered around the line but following the overall direction, which makes it seem like real data with noise. 



##Q2.6 (6 points): You’ve now found three irregularities: 1. The baseline distributions match an existing national survey 2. The re-test correlations are implausibly high (Study 1) 3. The within-person changes are implausibly small (Study 1) In 4–5 sentences, explain why these three findings together suggest the data were fabricated rather than collected in a real experiment. Use specific numbers from your analysis. Why would a fabricator produce data with these properties?
#Your interpretation: The baseline thermometer in study1 matches CCAP data in an extremely close way, with the exact shape of CCAP data in the visualization. In addition, the retest correlation in study1 is unrealistically high, and the variation in individual change in study1 is also suspiciously small (SD <2). Together, these patterns suggests that the data in study1 was fabricated. A fabricator would produce a dataset very similar to an existing dataset, with very small random noise and unrealistically perfect correlations with minimal individual variation.


```

```{r}


##3.1 Comparing Baselines Q3.1 (3 points): Filter gayreshaped.csv to Study 2. How many observations? Calculate the mean and SD of therm1 (baseline) for the treatment group and the control group separately.
# Your code here. There are 2441 observations. The mean and SD are shown below.

study2 <- gay_r |> 
  filter(study == 2)

nrow(study2)

study2 |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(
    mean = mean(therm1, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = sd(therm1, na.rm = TRUE)
  )


##Q3.2 (3 points): Create side-by-side histograms of Study 1 baseline (therm1) and Study 2 baseline (therm1), using the same bins as before. How do they compare visually? (2–3 sentences)
# Your code here

scatter_data <- gay_r |>
  filter(treatment == "No Contact", study %in% c(1,2))

ggplot(scatter_data,
       aes(x = therm1, y = therm2)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1,
              color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~study) +
  labs(
    x = "Thermometer Wave 1",
    y = "Thermometer Wave 2",
    title = "Retest Thermometer Responses"
  )


#Your interpretation: In study1, the scatterpoints lie perfectly along the line, indicating that therm2 is very identical to therm1. This is suspicious. In study2, the scatterpoints are dispersed around the line, looking like natural variation. So study2 has a pattern that looks more consistent and could be trusted.




##Q3.3 (3 points): Filter gayreshaped.csv to Study 2, keeping only the “Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser” and “No Contact” groups. Check balance at baseline: calculate the mean and SD of therm1 for each group and the standardized difference in means (same formula as Q1.5). Do the groups look balanced? (1–2 sentences)
# Your code here

study2_balance <- gay_r |>
  filter(study == 2,
         treatment %in% c("Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser",
                          "No Contact"))

study2_balance |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(
    mean_therm1 = mean(therm1, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_therm1 = sd(therm1, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

mean_gay <- mean(study2_balance$therm1[study2_balance$treatment ==
"Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser"], na.rm = TRUE)

mean_no <- mean(study2_balance$therm1[study2_balance$treatment ==
"No Contact"], na.rm = TRUE)

sd_gay <- sd(study2_balance$therm1[study2_balance$treatment ==
"Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser"], na.rm = TRUE)

sd_no <- sd(study2_balance$therm1[study2_balance$treatment ==
"No Contact"], na.rm = TRUE)

sd_diff <- (mean_gay - mean_no) / sqrt((sd_gay^2 + sd_no^2)/2)

sd_diff
#Your interpretation: The groups look balanced, because the mean and SD between therm1 and therm2 are almost the same and the standardized difference is only 0.05.



##Q3.4 (3 points): Now compare outcomes. Calculate the mean therm2 (post-conversation) for each group. What is the difference in post-period means (gay canvasser minus no contact)?
# Your code here
study2_balance |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(mean_therm2 = mean(therm2, na.rm = TRUE))

mean_gay_study2 <- mean(study2_balance$therm2[
  study2_balance$treatment == "Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser"
], na.rm = TRUE)

mean_no2 <- mean(study2_balance$therm2[
  study2_balance$treatment == "No Contact"
], na.rm = TRUE)

mean_gay_study2 - mean_no2



##Q3.5 (4 points): Calculate the standard error of the difference in post-period means from Q3.4.Does the CI include zero? What does this tell you about whether the groups differ after the conversations?
# Your code here

var_gay <- var(study2_balance$therm2[
  study2_balance$treatment == "Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser"
], na.rm = TRUE)

var_no <- var(study2_balance$therm2[
  study2_balance$treatment == "No Contact"
], na.rm = TRUE)

n_gay <- sum(study2_balance$treatment ==
"Same-Sex Marriage Script by Gay Canvasser")

n_no <- sum(study2_balance$treatment ==
"No Contact")

SE <- sqrt(var_gay/n_gay + var_no/n_no)

SE

diff <- mean_gay_study2 - mean_no2

lower <- diff - 1.96 * SE
upper <- diff + 1.96 * SE

lower
upper
#Your interpretation:The 95% CI does not include zero, which means that the groups significantly differ after conversation.



##Q3.6 (4 points): For the Study 2 control group, calculate the correlation between therm1 and each subsequent wave: therm2, therm3, and therm4. Use cor() with use = "complete.obs". Do the correlations decay over time (i.e., do more distant waves correlate less with baseline)? What does this pattern tell you about the stability of real survey measurements? (2–3 sentences)
# Your code here
#Your interpretation:


##Q3.7 (5 points): You’ve now analyzed fabricated data (Study 1), caught the fraud, and examined more realistic data (Study 2). In 5–6 sentences, discuss: • What makes Study 2’s data different from Study 1’s data? Be specific about which statistics differ and by how much.• Why is it important that real treatment effects are smaller and messier than fabricated ones? • What role did simple measurement tools (mean, SD, histograms, correlations) play in detecting the fraud? 
#Your reflection:


```
